coredata.dc:title: High-intensity ultrasound-assisted formation of cellulose nanofiber scaffold with low and high lignin content and their cytocompatibility with gingival fibroblast cells
coredata.dc:creator: Huerta, Raquel Razzera
Silva, Eric Keven
Ekaette, Idaresit
El-Bialy, Tarek
Saldaña, Marleny D.A.
coredata.dc:description: Cellulose nanofiber (CNF) hydrogels with low lignin (8%) (LL-CNF) and high lignin (18%) (HL-CNF) content were produced at nominal powers of 240, 720 and 1200 W using high-intensity ultrasound technology (HIUS). Freeze-dried CNF hydrogels were evaluated as scaffolds for gingival fibroblast cells proliferation aiming biomedical applications. HIUS processing improved the dispersibility of the CNF and increased the water retention value by more than 5 times. The LL-CNF had a maximum fibrillation yield of 46 wt.%, whereas the HL-CNF had a maximum fibrillation yield of 40 wt.% at nominal power of ≥720 W. Regardless of the lignin content, the CNF hydrogels exhibited a typical elastic gel-like behavior with the highest elasticity of 263 Pa. After freeze-drying, the CNF aerogels had porosity ≥ 96.8%, and swelling capacity up to 42.1 g PBS/g aerogel. Moreover, the cell proliferation assay showed no differences in proliferation among the LL-CNF and HL-CNF scaffolds up to 11 days. Therefore, CNF scaffolds prepared with lignin content up to 18% present promising application in the biomedical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Emerging technology
Ultrasonic efficiency
Acoustic cavitation phenomenon
Cellulose hydrogel
Cell viability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: HIUS-assisted production of cellulose nanofiber scaffolds
Cellulose nanofiber scaffolds characterization and cytocompatibility test
Cellulose nanofiber scaffolds characterization and cytocompatibility

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to acknowledge the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC, #04371-2019 ) for funding this research. Raquel R. Huerta thanks the Brazilian Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq) for the PhD scholarship. Eric Keven Silva thanks FAPESP (2018/14550-6) for his postdoctoral assistantship at University of Alberta. The authors also thank Dr. Samira Diar‑Bakirly, a former graduate student at University of Alberta, for providing the gingival fibroblast cells.

We would like to acknowledge the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC, #04371-2019) for funding this research. Raquel R. Huerta thanks the Brazilian Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq) for the PhD scholarship. Eric Keven Silva thanks FAPESP (2018/14550-6) for his postdoctoral assistantship at University of Alberta. The authors also thank Dr. Samira Diar?Bakirly, a former graduate student at University of Alberta, for providing the gingival fibroblast cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and diameter size distribution of 1 wt.% LL-CNF and HL-CNF obtained using HIUS at acoustic powers of 53 and 51 W, respectively.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of 1 wt.% LL-CNF aerogel (A) and HL-CNF aerogel (B) obtained using HIUS at acoustic powers of 53 and 51 W, respectively. Blue arrows show the nanofibers aggregation after the freezing step. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Biocompatibility test of control (glass slip), LL-CNF and HL-CNF aerogels (1–2 wt.%) on gingival fibroblast cells cultured for 11 days. a–fDifferent lowercase letters indicate significant differences (p-value < 0.05). (B) Cell viability of LL-CNF and HL-CNF aerogels at day 11.

Stained scaffold sections of LL-CNF aerogel (A) and HL-CNF aerogel (B) after incubation for 11 days. The pink arrows and the dark purple stain indicate the cell nuclei. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Characterization and visual appearance of high lignin (HL) and low lignin (LL) initial cellulosic fibers obtained after 2 and 6 h of bleaching, respectively.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Contents are expressed as mean ± standard deviation of at least duplicates.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nominal and acoustic 1 powers, HIUS efficiency 1 and intensity 1 , and the initial and final temperature recorded during HIUS processing of CNF (1 wt.%) suspensions.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated using Eqs. (1–3). Contents are expressed as mean ± standard deviation of duplicates.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Water retention value (WRV), zeta (ζ) potential, fibrillation yield and visual appearance of LL-CNF and HL-CNF (1 wt.%) suspensions treated using HIUS at different nominal and acoustic powers.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: HIUS: High-intensity ultrasound; LL-CNF: low lignin cellulose nanofiber; HL-CNF: high lignin cellulose nanofiber. Contents are expressed as mean ± standard deviation of duplicates. a–fDifferent lowercase letters in the same column indicate significant differences (p-value < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Power law parameters for elastic (G′) and viscous (G″) modulus.

and

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of LL-CNF and HL-CNF aerogel properties obtained by freeze drying.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Contents are expressed as mean ± standard deviation duplicates. a–cDifferent lowercase letters in the same column indicate significant differences (p-value < 0.05).

head.ce:title: High-intensity ultrasound-assisted formation of cellulose nanofiber scaffold with low and high lignin content and their cytocompatibility with gingival fibroblast cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Agricultural, Food and Nutritional Science, University of Alberta, Edmonton, AB T6G 2P5, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Agricultural, Food and Nutritional Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Agricultural, Food and Nutritional Science, University of Alberta, Edmonton, AB T6G 2P5, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Medicine and Dentistry, University of Alberta, Edmonton, AB T6G 2E1, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Medicine and Dentistry, University of Alberta, Edmonton, AB T6G 2P5, Canada

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: 3-18A Agriculture/Forestry Ctr, 115 St – 90 Ave, Edmonton, AB T6G 2P5, Canada.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellulose nanofiber (CNF) hydrogels with low lignin (8%) (LL-CNF) and high lignin (18%) (HL-CNF) content were produced at nominal powers of 240, 720 and 1200 W using high-intensity ultrasound technology (HIUS). Freeze-dried CNF hydrogels were evaluated as scaffolds for gingival fibroblast cells proliferation aiming biomedical applications. HIUS processing improved the dispersibility of the CNF and increased the water retention value by more than 5 times. The LL-CNF had a maximum fibrillation yield of 46 wt.%, whereas the HL-CNF had a maximum fibrillation yield of 40 wt.% at nominal power of ≥720 W. Regardless of the lignin content, the CNF hydrogels exhibited a typical elastic gel-like behavior with the highest elasticity of 263 Pa. After freeze-drying, the CNF aerogels had porosity ≥ 96.8%, and swelling capacity up to 42.1 g PBS/g aerogel. Moreover, the cell proliferation assay showed no differences in proliferation among the LL-CNF and HL-CNF scaffolds up to 11 days. Therefore, CNF scaffolds prepared with lignin content up to 18% present promising application in the biomedical field.

ce:section.ce:para: Canola is one of the most important oilseed crops in Canada and worldwide. After harvesting the seeds, canola generates large quantities of straw that has been recently used to obtain fermentable sugars [1], and phenolic compounds and carbohydrates [2]. The isolation of cellulose nanofibers (CNF) from agricultural biomass, including straw is gaining increased attention mainly due to their high availability and low cost [3–8]. CNF is characterized by a three-dimensional (3D) network of fibrils with diameters in the nano-scale (<100 nm) and length of several microns that have unique properties such as biodegradability, high specific strength and stiffness, high aspect ratio and surface area and light weight.

Particularly, CNF hydrogels and aerogels create remarkable scaffolds for cell culture due to its outstanding mechanical stability, biodegradability, controllable nano-morphology and high porosity [9]. CNF scaffolds showed non-cytotoxicity in vitro and biocompatibility in vivo [10–12]. Furthermore, CNF scaffolds overcome the poor stability and mechanical properties of other natural polymers such as chitosan and alginate, and overcome the potential immunogenicity and disease transmission concerns by using synthetic and animal derived polymeric matrices [13,14].

CNF have been commonly prepared by mechanical nanofibrillation via grinding, high pressure homogenization and microfluidization. However, nanofibrillation using those techniques requires several passes, and a common problem associated with high-pressure homogenization is clogging, which requires a pre-treatment using a mechanical approach to reduce fiber size or other alternative is to use low cellulose concentration solution [15,16]. Meanwhile, the use of high-intensity ultrasound (HIUS) processing, a clean and emerging technology, has shown high performance on the nanofibrillation process of cellulosic materials such as canola straw, lupin hull and wood, due to the acoustic cavitation phenomenon [3,5,17]. This phenomenon occurs due to the application of low-frequency (20-100 kHz) and high-power ultrasound in a liquid medium, promoting the formation and subsequent collapse of microbubbles associated with extreme levels of highly localized turbulence and shear stress [18], which promotes the nanofibrillation.

Although the recent production of CNF from agriculture biomass containing residual non-cellulosic materials like lignin and without surface modification have attracted attention due to the low cost of the starting material and simple procedure [3,4,6–8], only two researchers have studied the cytocompatibility of cellulose with residual lignin scaffold [7,8]. Nakasone and Kobayashi [7] evaluated the cytocompatibility of cellulose hydrogel films with residual lignin of 0.6-1.6% obtained from sugarcane bagasse. After 3 days, mouse fibroblast cell density reached 4 × 104 cell/cm2 for cellulose hydrogels with 0.6% lignin and 6 × 104 cell/cm2 for cellulose hydrogels containing 1.6% lignin. However, the amount of lignin is still considerable low, and impartial to conclude its real cytocompatibility. Later, Quraishi et al. [19] evaluated the cytocompatibility of lignin isolated from wheat straw in alginate-based aerogel composite as scaffold for mouse fibroblast cells. Aerogels with 2:1 (w/w) alginate-to-lignin ratio showed no cytotoxicity and promoted satisfactory cells adhesion and viability over 7 days compared to the control (tissue culture in the polystyrene plate) [19]. However, the cellulose fiber was not used in this case.

Although lignin from agriculture biomass has shown to be non-cytotoxic, there is scarce literature using CNF with high lignin for in vitro tests. Therefore, we aim to evaluate the HIUS-assisted formation of CNF scaffolds containing low (8%) and high (18%) residual lignin contents and test their cytocompatibility with gingival fibroblast cells for periodontal therapy. In regenerative periodontal therapy, a major challenge is reconstruction of morphology and function of gingival tissues [20]. To obtain the CNF scaffold, bleached cellulose fibers with different lignin contents were first nanofibrillated using HIUS at 240, 720 and 1200 W to obtain hydrogels, and further freeze-dried to form aerogels scaffolds. The CNF suspensions and hydrogels were characterized for morphology, water retention value, surface charge density, fibrillation yield, and viscoelastic properties. The aerogels were characterized for porosity, swelling capacity, morphology, and cytocompatibility.

All solvents and reagents used in this study were analytical grade. Sodium chlorite, acetic acid, and formaldehyde solution (36.5-38%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Oakville, ON, Canada) and used without further purification. Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin-streptomycin, trypsin, and phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) were obtained from Fischer Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). AlamarBlue® cell proliferation assay was purchased from Bio-Rad (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA). The Type 1 water used was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

Canola straw cellulose fibers were obtained via pressurized aqueous ethanol treatment at 180 °C, 50 bar, 20% (v/v) ethanol, 5 mL/min, and 40 min [2], followed by bleaching using acidified sodium chlorite at 75 °C for 2 and 6 h [21]. Low lignin (LL) cellulose fiber was obtained after 6 h of bleaching, resulting in 82% cellulose, 4% hemicellulose, and 8% lignin. Whereas high lignin (HL) cellulose fiber was obtained after 2 h of bleaching treatment, resulting in 71% cellulose, 5% hemicellulose, 18% lignin and other minor components (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: HIUS-assisted production of cellulose nanofiber scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para:
The LL-CNF and HL-CNF suspensions/hydrogels were obtained by mechanical disintegration of cellulose fibers using the HIUS equipment (Model FS-1200 N, Shanghai Sonxi Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Shanghai, ZJ, China). For the HIUS treatment, the cellulose fibers were soaked in Type 1 water (1 wt.%) and treated using a 20-mm diameter probe at 20 kHz in a continuous mode [3]. The sonication was performed at nominal power levels of 240, 720 and 1200 W for 8 min. The acoustic power was obtained according to the calorimetric assay using Eq. (1) [22]:
(1)
Acoustic
power
W
= m
C
P
dT
dt
where, m is the sample mass (g),
C P
is the specific heat of the sample (LL-CNF had
C P
of 4.34 J/g°C and HL-CNF had
C P
of 4.06 J/g°C) and (dT/dt) is the change in temperature over time (°C s−1). The
C P
values for each suspension of LL-CNF and HL-CNF were obtained by differential scanning calorimetry analysis using DSC Q100 (TA instruments, New Castle, DE, USA) (see Fig. S1 of the supporting information).

HIUS efficiency and intensity were calculated according to Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively [23], using the same nominal powers of 240, 720 and 1200 W.
(2)
HIUS
efficiency
%
=
Acoustic
power
( W )
Nominal
power
W
× 100
(3)
HIUS
intensity
W / c
m
2
=
4 × Acoustic
power
π
D
2
where,
D
(cm) is the probe diameter.

Then, the HL-CNF and LL-CNF (1-2 wt.%) hydrogels (10 g) were placed in cylindrical tubes (8 cm height and 1.6 cm diameter) to create the scaffold monoliths, frozen overnight at -18 °C, and dried at 45 °C, 15 Pa for 3 days using a freeze dryer (FreeZone, Labco Corp., Kansas, MO, USA).

Gingival fibroblast cells were obtained from healthy donors, the protocol of obtaining these cells has been approved by the University of Alberta Human ethics committee (protocol # MS6_Pro00056111) used for the in vitro culture on HL-CNF and LL-CNF aerogel scaffolds. The cells at passage 3 were grown in culture flasks (T-75 cm2) containing Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetalbovineserum (FBS), and 5% (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin and incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 and saturated water atmosphere. After the cells achieved approximately 90% of confluence, the cells were harvested viatrypsinization. The number of viable cells in suspension was estimated by counting in theBio-RadTC20™ automatedcell counter (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA).

The HL-CNF and LL-CNF scaffolds (10 mm height and 18 mm diameter) and the control (glass cover slips) were placed in 24-well culture plates and sterilized using ethanol solutions from 10 to 70% (v/v) [24]. Briefly, the scaffolds were soaked with 1.5 mL of 10% (v/v) ethanol solution for 30 min, and gradually increased up to 70% (v/v) ethanol at a rate of 20% (v/v) ethanol concentration increase. Then, the scaffolds were reversed washed from 70 to 10% (v/v) ethanol at the same rate and time described above. Finally, the sterile scaffolds were washed with sterile Hanks’ Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS) at pH 7.4 for three times (30 min each). Then the scaffolds were soaked in 600 μL DMEM and incubated overnight at 37 °C under 5% CO2 water saturated atmosphere. Quadruplicate of each scaffold were seeded with 50 μL gingival fibroblast cells suspension (1.6 × 106 cells/mL) with a micropipette on the center of the scaffolds and incubated for 4 h to allow cell adhesion. The scaffolds were washed gently with sterile HBSS solution to remove non-attached cells and transferred to a new sterile 24-well culture plates containing 600 μL of DMEM and the seeded face of the scaffold submerged in the media. The media was replaced every other day up to 11 days.

The water retention value (WRV) of HL-CNF and LL-CNF (1 wt.%) suspensions was determined according to the methodology described by Gu et al. [25], with slight modifications. Briefly, the dispersion samples were first centrifuged at 900 g and 25 °C for 30 min (AccuSpin 400, Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Then, the supernatant was discarded and the wet solid samples (Wwet) were oven dried at approximately 105 °C until reaching a constant mass (Wdry), and the WRV was calculated using Eq. (4):
(4)
W R V
g / g
=
W
w e t
-
W
d r y
W
d r y

For the determination of zeta (ζ) potential, the HL-CNF and LL-CNF suspensions were analyzed using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, WMD, UK). Briefly, the suspension was diluted to 0.1% (v/v), placed inside the capillary zeta cell cuvette and scanned. A total of five scans were performed for each sample at 25 °C. The results are shown as an average of the scans.

The fibrillation yield was calculated via nanofibril content according to the procedure described by Jiang et al. [6]. Briefly, the diluted (0.2 wt.%) HL-CNF and LL-CNF suspensions were centrifuged at 1800 g for 20 min. Then, the supernatant was discarded and the sediment (non-nanofibrillated and partially nanofibrillated fibrils with larger size) was dried at 100 °C until constant mass. The nanofibril content was calculated using to Eq. (5):
(5)
N a n o f i b r i l
c o n t e n t
w t %
=
1 -
W e i g h t
o f
d r i e d
s e d i m e n t
g
W e i g h t
o f
t h e
c e l l u l o s i c
f i b e r
b e f o r e
c e n t r i f u g a t i o n
( g )
× c e l l u l o s e
c o n t e n t × 100

For the diameter size distribution, images of the HL-CNF and LL-CNF suspensions were obtained using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (H7500 TEM, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). First, the suspensions were diluted to 0.01% (v/v) and one drop of each dilution was placed onto square mesh TEM grids. To obtain a better contrast of the nanofibers, the sample was negatively stained with 1% uranyl acetate for one minute at 25 °C. From the images obtained, the diameter of the nanofibers was calculated using the ImageJ software. For each sample, a total of 100 measurements were done within 10 images.

The viscoelastic behaviors of HL-CNF and LL-CNF (1-2 wt.%) hydrogels were studied using a rotational rheometer (Physica MCR 302, Anton Paar, Ashland, VA, USA). Strain sweep test was performed to determine the linear viscoelastic region within a strain range from 0.01 to 1000% at an angular frequency of 6.28 rads/s (1 Hz). Angular frequency sweep measurements were conducted in a frequency range of 1–100 rads/s at 1.0% strain. Data were analyzed using the RheoPlus software, and the results are shown as storage modulus (G′), loss modulus (G″), and complex viscosity (
η
∗
).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellulose nanofiber scaffolds characterization and cytocompatibility test
ce:section.ce:para:
The weight of the HL-CNF and LL-CNF aerogels was determined using a sensitive electronic balance (ME104, Mettler-Toledo, Columbus, OH, USA) with a precision of 0.1 mg and the final dimensions were measured using a caliper with a precision of 0.05 mm. Then, the bulk density (da) values were obtained by calculating the ratio of the mass to the volume and the final internal porosity (P%) of the aerogels was calculated using to Eq. (6), where the density of crystalline CNF (dn) is equal to 1.6 g/cm3 [17]:
(6)
P
%
=
1 -
d a
d n
× 100

The swelling capacity of the HL-CNF and LL-CNF aerogels was determined by immersion in a phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) at 25 °C for 24 h [24]. After the immersion time, the excess of PBS was removed gently with filter paper and the swollen scaffold was weighted. The swelling capacity (g/g) was calculated by the mass difference of dry and swollen scaffold.

Cross sections of CNF aerogels were carbon coated using Nanotek SEMprep 2 sputter coater (Nanotech, Manchester, UK) for scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis. Samples were scanned at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV using a Zeiss Sigma 300 VP-FESEM equipment (Carl Zeiss AG, Oberkochen, BW, Germany).

The cell growth was quantified after 1, 3, 7 and 11 days of incubation using the alamarBlue® cell proliferation assay (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA). The assay incorporates a reduction-oxidation (REDOX) indicator that both fluoresces and undergoes colorimetric change in response to cellular metabolic reduction. The amount of fluorescence produced is proportional to the number of living cells. Briefly, the seeded scaffolds were cultured with 10% (v/v) alamarBlue reagent and incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 4 h. Then, the fluorescence of the reduced media containing alamarBlue was measured at 560 nm excitation wavelength and 590 nm emission wavelength and the alamarBlue Fluorometric calculator (https://www.bio-rad-antibodies.com/colorimetric-calculator-fluorometric-alamarBlue.html#) was used to measure cell respiration as an indicator for proliferation and cytotoxicity.

After 11 days of cell culture, the scaffolds were fixed with 4% (v/v) formaldehyde in PBS solution at 25 °C for 24 h for cell imaging. Briefly, the scaffolds were dehydrated gradually in ethanol/water and embedded in paraffin. A cross section (~7 μm) located 1 mm depth from the surface was mounted on glass slides, stained with hematoxylin and eosin, and images were taken using a brightfield microscope (Zeiss Primo Vert, Carl Zeiss AG, Oberkochen, BW, Germany).

All experiments were carried out in duplicate and all values were reported as mean values ± standard deviation. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed using Minitab 18® Software (Minitab Inc., State College, PA, USA). The significant differences (p-value < 0.05) between the treatments were evaluated using Tukey’s procedure.

Table 1 shows the compositional analysis of the cellulosic fibers used in this study. HL cellulosic fiber had mainly 71% cellulose, 5% hemicellulose and 18% lignin, whereas the LL cellulosic fiber had mainly 82% cellulose, 4% hemicellulose and 8% lignin. Protein, ash and moisture contents were similar for both cellulosic fibers. Also, the visual appearance of the cellulosic fibers showed that a single bleaching application (2 h) had a limited effect on the fiber brightness, leaving a yellow color to the HL fiber. However, with increased bleaching process time up to 6 h and consecutive inter-washings, large amounts of lignin were removed, improving the brightness of the LL fiber.

The HIUS treatment was then used to disintegrate the initial cellulosic fibers into LL-CNF and HL-CNF. Table 2 summarizes the nominal and acoustic powers, HIUS efficiency and intensity, as well as the initial and final process temperature recorded. The acoustic power dissipated into the system had slight variations upon different CNF used, mainly due to the different specific heat (
C P
) of the nanofibers. The LL-CNF had
C P
of 4.34 J/g°C and during the HIUS treatment the acoustic power varied from 26 to 53 W. Meanwhile, HL-CNF had
C P
of 4.06 J/g°C, and during the HIUS treatment the acoustic power varied from 21 to 51 W (Table 2).

Increasing the nominal power from 240 to 1200 W resulted in a decrease on HIUS efficiency from 11 to 4% in the LL-CNF system, and from 9 to 4% in the HL-CNF system (Table 2). According to Gogate et al. [23], the electric power energy (nominal power) is first converted into mechanical energy, then acoustical energy (acoustic power), and finally into cavitation and heat energies. Therefore, as the HIUS efficiency (%) is the relation between acoustic power and nominal power (Eq. (2)), the low HIUS efficiency was mainly due to the greater acoustical energy dissipation during the energy conversion stages. It is known that the energy is dissipated in different ways during each energy exchange process. However, the low efficiency can also be attributed to the acoustic decoupling effect, which changed the acoustic impedance as the power increased, and the coalescence effect, which led to increased number of bubbles around the transducer surface absorbing/scattering the mechanical energy [26]. Also, by increasing the nominal power and, consequently, the acoustic streaming, a high turbulence inside the system lowered the cavitation effects [27]. Then, the HIUS efficiency was also affected by the increased process temperature (Table 2). Increasing the nominal power from 240 to 1200 W, resulted in an increase temperature from 55 to 94 °C on the LL-CNF system, and from 51 to 91 °C on the HL-CNF system, which led to an increased vapour pressure of the system, reducing the gas content of the bubbles and minimizing the cavitation energy [22]. As it is known, during the cavitation phenomenon, microbubbles are created within the liquid medium that oscillates from expansion and compression phases. The microbubble collapses as cavitation energy, when the amount of gas that diffuses into the bubbles during the expansion phase is greater than the amount of gas that diffuses out of the microbubble during the compression phase [28].

Recently, Mamvura et al. [29] studied the energy change from electrical to acoustical to cavitation, and the efficiency of conversion rate from one form of energy to another during HIUS treatment. The acoustical energy was calculated as the acoustic power calculated herein, and the cavitation energy was calculated by the difference of calorimetric energy in both cavitating and non-cavitating system. The conversion efficiency from electrical to acoustical energy decreased from 26 to 25% as the nominal power increased from 38 to 180 W. And, the conversion efficiency from acoustical to cavitation energy decreased from 11 to 7%, with the same nominal power rate increase [29]. The conversion efficiency from electrical to acoustical energy was higher than those observed in the current study (7–17%), due to the lower nominal power used by Mamvura et al [29]. Although several authors have used HIUS processing to obtain CNF [3,5,17], to the best of our knowledge, there is no study that reported the acoustic power dissipated in the system for an accurate cavitation/efficiency calculation.

The effects of applied ultrasonic power on water retention value (WRV), surface charge density (ζ potential) and fibrillation yield of LL-CNF and HL-CNF are shown in Table 3 . Both the lignin content of CNF and power applied significantly (p-value < 0.0001) affected the WRV of the nanofibers. The maximum WRV obtained with the HL-CNF was 34 g water/g CNF, while the LL-CNF had a WRV up to 82 g water/g CNF. Since the LL-CNF was bleached for longer time, a major portion of the lignin material could be oxidized, creating larger void volumes within the cellulose fibers, which increased the surface area and water absorption capacity of those nanofibers. Furthermore, as a consequence of the increased nominal/acoustic power and the cavitation effects, the surface area of nanofibers was further increased, positively affecting the WRV (Table 3). When the cellulose fiber was fragmented into thinner fibrils and its surface area increased, a greater exposure of the hydroxyl (OH) groups enhanced the hydrogen bonding within water molecules, which improved the WRV capacity of the LL-CNF and HL-CNF.

Besides the WRV, the ζ potential and fibrillation yield were also assessed to evaluate the impact of applied ultrasonic power and lignin content of the CNF during the HIUS process. As the ζ potential measures the magnitude of surface charge of the nanofibers, it can describe the aggregation and dispersion stability of the suspension. However, in the present study, the ζ potential was not affected by the ultrasonic power used (p-value = 0.136) or the lignin content of CNF (p-value = 0.386). According to Sato et al. [30], the aggregation and dispersion stability of CNF are mainly affected by the solution chemistry and surface modification. As in this study, we avoided using any additional chemical for surface modification and all CNF were dispersed in water, they had similar ζ potential values, ranging between -30 and -36 mV.

The applied ultrasonic power (p-value = 0.027) and lignin content of CNF (p-value < 0.0001) significantly affected the fibrillation yield. Increasing the ultrasonic acoustic power from 21-26 to 51-53 W increased the fibrillation yield of LL-CNF from 42 to 46 wt.%, and of HL-CNF from 36 to 40 wt.%, due to the increased cavitation phenomenon. Table 3 also shows the visual appearance of the CNF solution, which represents a qualitative analysis of the nanofibrillation process using the HIUS technology. Independent of the lignin content, the HIUS process at acoustic power of 21-26 W resulted in a heterogeneous solution due to the incomplete nanofibrillation achieved at this level of cavitation. A further increase on the power to 51-53 W resulted in well dispersed, highly nanofibrillated fibers, that self-assembled as hydrogel structures.

Further, the TEM images confirm that independent of the lignin content, the HIUS treatment at acoustic power of 51-53 W successfully nanofibrillated the initial cellulosic material, producing an entangled micro/nanofibrillated network (Fig. 1 ). According to Wang et al. [31], the nanofibrillation of cellulose starts by breaking down a “backbone” micron size structural fibril, which is highly compact and present a large diameter, into submicron and then nanoscale fibrils. The presence of “backbone” structural fibrils can still be observed in both samples, but more “backbone” fibrils were noticed on the HL-CNF hydrogels, indicating a lower nanofibrillation of this sample. The recalcitrant property of the HL-CNF to nanofibrillation is due to the stiff complex formed between cellulose and lignin, which required long mechanical treatment to be disintegrated. The higher nanofibrillation of LL-CNF was further confirmed by the size distribution calculated using the TEM images. For each type of CNF studied, the obtained nanofiber diameters were segregated in groups of diameters ranging between 0-10 and >50 nm, where their relative frequency is plotted in Fig. 1. The HL-CNF hydrogel had nanofibers with diameters of 20-30 nm in a relative frequency of 23% and nanofibers bundles (>50 nm) in a relative low frequency of 3%, accounting for an average diameter size of 18 nm. In comparison, the LL-CNF hydrogel had an average diameter size of 14 nm, due to the small diameter of the nanofibers (43% of the nanofibers had diameter of <10 nm). Therefore, the results herein presented confirm that the higher WRV and fibrillation yield obtained by the LL-CNF compared to the HL-CNF was due to the higher aspect ratio and surface area of the LL-CNF.

According to Fig. 2A and C, for strain less than 1%, all the CNF hydrogels had G′ basically independent of the applied strain (LVR). The
γ c
value of CNF hydrogels was defined by the CNF solid concentration and the residual lignin content. A decrease on the
γ c
value of the hydrogels was observed when the CNF concentration increased from 1 to 2 wt.% for both LL-CNF and HL-CNF hydrogels. The HL-CNF hydrogel at a lower solid concentration (1 wt.%) had the highest
γ c
value of 4.0%, whereas the increase on CNF concentration to 1.5 and 2 wt.% resulted in a decreased on the
γ c
values to 2.5 and 1.6%, respectively. Similar behavior was observed for LL-CNF hydrogel. The
γ c
value of the 1–1.5 wt.% LL-CNF hydrogel was 1.6%, whereas when the concentration increased up to 2 wt.%, the
γ c
value of the LL-CNF hydrogel decreased to 1.0%. According to Yue et al. [32], the lower the
γ c
value, the shorter the LVR, resulting in an increased rigidity of the hydrogel.

Fig. 2B and D shows the rheological behavior of the LL-CNF and HL-CNF hydrogels, respectively, obtained by angular frequency sweep test at 1% strain. Regardless of the lignin content and CNF solid concentration, all samples exhibited a typical elastic gel-like behavior, as the G′ values were all higher than G″ values in the frequency range of 1-100 rad/s. The LL-CNF and HL-CNF hydrogels at 1 wt.% solid concentration had G′ values of 93 and 38, respectively. Whereas the increase on solid concentration from 1 to 1.5 wt.% of the HL-CNF hydrogel did not result in any change on the G′ value. The HL-CNF hydrogel at 2 wt.% had an increased G′ value of 123 Pa. For the LL-CNF hydrogels, the G′ value increased simultaneously with the increase of the solid concentration, where the maximum G′ value was 264 Pa at 2 wt.% solid concentration. Further, the power law model was applied to obtain better understanding of the interdependence of
ω
and dynamic modulus (Table 4 ).

The power law model is described by
G ' =
a 1
ω
b 1
and
G ' ' =
a 2
ω
b 2
, where
a 1
and
a 2
are the magnitudes of G′ and G′′ at a given angular frequency (
ω
), and
b 1
and
b 2
are the relative degrees of viscoelasticity of the gels [33]. According to Table 4, the values of
a 1
were greater than the values corresponding to
a 2
at
ω
of 1–10 rad/s, indicating that the elastic responses were greater than the viscous for both CNF hydrogels. Also, the values for
b 1
were greater than those of
b 2
at
ω
of 10-100 rad/s, indicating that even at high frequency the prepared CNF hydrogels had solid-like behavior with a comparatively small dissipation in energy. It also indicated that the hydrogels had frequency dependence, as
b 1
and
b 2
increases towards higher
ω
. The
b 1
values were between 0.12 and 1.21 for the LL-CNF hydrogels, and 0.15-1.53 for the HL-CNF hydrogels at
ω
of 1-100 rad/s. The lower
b 1
values of the LL-CNF hydrogels compared to the HL-CNF hydrogels at all concentrations studied, indicated an intensive entanglement of the nanofiber, higher strength and stability of the hydrogel, and less deformation with increasing
ω
. The increased stability and mechanical strength of the LL-CNF hydrogel can be associated to the higher degree of nanofibrillation and WRV of the LL-CNF hydrogel compared to the HL-CNF hydrogel. As mentioned, the high degree of nanofibrillation obtained with the LL-CNF promoted greater aspect ratio of the nanofibers and increased WRV of the hydrogel network (Table 3). As a result, the physical entanglements between nanofibers, and hydrogen bonding between nanofibers and water molecules increased, promoting high stability and strength to the LL-CNF hydrogels.

The complex viscosity (
η
∗
) plotted against the angular frequency is shown in Fig. 2E. According to Tanaka et al. [34], as the aspect ratio of nanocelluloses increases, they become more flexible and their suspension network tends to have a higher viscosity. This agrees with the results obtained herein. Higher aspect ratio of the LL-CNF resulted in higher
η
∗
at 1 rad/s (96-270 Pa.s) compared to HL-CNF (40-125 Pa.s). Furthermore, all CNF suspensions had a decline on the
η
∗
as the
ω
increased, showing a typical shear-thinning behavior [35]. According to Moberg et al. [36], the shear-thinning behavior of elastic CNF suspensions is expected since the nanofibrils tend to orient themselves in a network when no forces are applied, but under external deformation, the physical interaction are dissembled, increasing flow of the suspensions and decreasing the viscosity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellulose nanofiber scaffolds characterization and cytocompatibility
ce:section.ce:para: During the freezing step, the HL-CNF dispersed in water were segregated by the ice growth and moved together, triggering large fiber walls as shown in the blue arrows in Fig. 3B. These nanofiber aggregations led to shrinkage after sublimation (Table 5). Since the LL-CNF were more homogenously distributed on the hydrogel complex (Fig. 1), they were also more homogenously distributed during the ice growth (Fig. 3A). This fact assisted the structural integrity and diameter of LL-CNF aerogel after sublimation. Earlier, Martoïa et al. [37] used TEMPO-oxidized and enzymatic treated CNF to obtain aerogels. The CNF aerogels from the TEMPO-oxidized process exhibited pores with more regular geometry and size than those of the enzymatic process due to the smallest dimension and the highest aspect ratio of the TEMPO-oxidized nanofibers, which were more homogeneously redistributed during the ice growth procedure [37].

As the density of the aerogel is inversely correlated to the porosity (Eq. (6)), the highest porosity was calculated as 99.4% for the LL-CNF aerogel at 1 wt.% concentration with a density of 0.009 g/cm3. With the increase of initial CNF concentration to 1.5 wt.%, and the increase of density to 0.019 g/cm3, the LL-CNF aerogel had a porosity of 98.7%. The trend and values herein obtained were compatible with Ciftci et al. [5], who obtained highly purified CNF aerogels (1-2 wt.%) from treated lupin hull (~97% cellulose) via HIUS at 560 W and supercritical CO2 (SCCO2) drying at 40 °C, 100 bar, and flow rate of 0.5 L/min (measured at ambient conditions) for 4 h. The aerogels at a concentration of 1 wt.% had density of 0.009 g/cm3 and porosity of 99.4%, whereas CNF aerogels at a concentration of 1.5 wt.% had increased density of 0.019 g/cm3 and lower porosity of 98.8%. It is worth mentioning that Ciftci et al. [5] also dried the obtained CNF hydrogels using freeze drying, the same method used in our study. Using freeze drying, the CNFs aerogel at 1 wt.% and 1.5 wt.% had porosities of 98.6% and 98.1%, respectively, which values were lower than the LL-CNF aerogels herein obtained (99.4% and 98.4%, respectively). This can be attributed to the lower nominal power applied by Ciftci et al. [5] (560 W) compared to the used in this study (1200 W). Furthermore, CNF aerogels obtained herein using HIUS processing had higher porosity than that of obtained via high-pressure homogenizer at 1000 bar which had porosity of 93% [12].

Table 5 also shows the swelling capacity of the LL-CNF and HL-CNF aerogels in PBS (pH 7.4) at room temperature after 24 h immersion time. The LL-CNF aerogels had maximum swelling capacity of 42.1 g PBS/g aerogel, whereas the HL-CNF had maximum swelling capacity of 26.2 g PBS/g aerogel. This trend was similar to the WRV responses of the HL-CNF and LL-CNF hydrogels (Table 3), whereas the high lignin content reduced the absorption capacity of the hydrogels. As lignin is hydrophobic in nature, HL-CNF aerogels/hydrogels are expected to have lower swelling capacity and WRV than LL-CNFs samples. Furthermore, it can be inferred that a high porosity structure of the LL-CNF aerogels induced a fast and increased PBS uptake ability than the HL-CNF aerogels. Rashad et al. [12] obtained similar swelling capacities of 25.2 and 25.0 g PBS/g aerogel for TEMPO-oxidized and carboxymethylated CNF aerogels (1 wt.%), respectively, after 24 h immersion time. However, bacterial CNF aerogel showed a lower swelling capacity of 12 g PBS/g aerogel, and disintegrated within 2 h of immersion time [38]. Higher swelling capacity of CNF aerogel obtained herein and by Rashad et al. [12] has enhanced application as wound healing scaffold.

To further explore the use of these LL-CNF and HL-CNF aerogels for biomedical applications, the first step was to test the material cytocompatibility, which must be as high as possible to eliminate any adverse effect within the living tissue or microbial contamination. In this study, gingival fibroblast cells were used for the evaluation of cell adhesion, viability and proliferation of the LL-CNF and HL-CNF scaffolds (1-2 wt.%) obtained using ultrasound at acoustic powers of 51-53 W (Fig. 4 ). Within day 1, the LL-CNF and HL-CNF scaffolds demonstrated similar cell adhesion as the control (glass slip) (Fig. 4A). This suggests that both of these surfaces can further stimulate human gingival fibroblast cell proliferation with no cytotoxicity. However, throughout the 11 days, the LL-CNF and HL-CNF scaffolds significantly increased the cell proliferation rate of the fibroblast cells compared to the control (Fig. 4A). At day 11, the cell viability of CNF scaffolds reached a maximum of 236% with no statistical difference (p-value = 0.960) between scaffolds prepared using different CNF solid content (Fig. 4B). This increased cell viability and proliferation of prepared CNF scaffolds are probably due to the 3D morphology, which allowed the cells to proliferate not only along the surface but also inside its internal structure. In case of the control (a 2D glass slip), the cells grew only on the surface of the glass, which reduced the cell proliferation rate. However, a different behavior was reported by Chiaoprakobkij et al. [38] using a 3D scaffold from bacterial CNF. The proliferation test on gingival fibroblast cells showed that on day 2 (the longest day analyzed), the percentage of relative cell viability on bacterial CNF scaffolds were lower than those of the control (2D tissue culture in polystyrene plate). Therefore, our CNF scaffold showed outstanding ability to support cell growth over the control, and it also had better performance than the scaffold from bacterial CNF [38].

On day 3, no statistical difference (p-value = 0.653) of cell proliferation within the LL-CNF scaffolds was observed. However, the HL-CNF scaffold at 1 wt.% had higher cell population compared to the HL-CNF scaffold at 2 wt.%. It is possible that the higher cell growth rate observed on the HL-CNF scaffold at a lower concentration may be related to the higher porosity compared to the same scaffold at 2 wt.% (Table 4). Furthermore, the statistical difference between LL-CNF and HL-CNF scaffolds at 1 wt.% may be related to the induced segregation of the HL-CNF scaffold walls compared to the LL-CNF, which provided stiffer and larger substrate area for the cell proliferation (Fig. 3). From day 3 to day 11, no statistical difference on cell population was observed for each group of CNF scaffold at any specific concentration (p-value = 0.460). However, according to Fig. 5 , the HL-CNF scaffold preserved most of its porosity and showed less rupture on its walls compared to the LL-CNF scaffolds. It is likely that in a long term, the HL-CNF scaffolds can induce favorable cell-scaffold interaction due to its morphology and possible higher mechanical properties. But, further studies are ongoing in our laboratory to confirm this statement.

The HIUS processing demonstrated to be an effective approach for successful production of CNF scaffolds with low (8%) and high (18%) residual lignin contents. The HIUS treatment induced the self-assembling process of CNF into hydrogels (1-2 wt.%) without the need of surface modification or crosslinkers. When freeze dried, the CNF aerogels with 18% lignin content had thicker cell walls and lower swelling capacity than the CNF scaffolds with 8% lignin content, mainly due to the hydrophobicity of the nanofibers. However, the cytocompatibility assay indicates that the residual lignin on CNF scaffolds is nontoxic and the prepared scaffolds induced higher human gingival cell viability and proliferation than the control. For the first time, CNF scaffolds with residual lignin content of 18%, and without surface modification were reported as a promising material for biomedical applications. Thereby, the use of CNF scaffold with residual non-cellulosic materials like lignin may open new opportunities for the application of nanocelluloses in the biomedical field.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to acknowledge the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC, #04371-2019) for funding this research. Raquel R. Huerta thanks the Brazilian Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq) for the PhD scholarship. Eric Keven Silva thanks FAPESP (2018/14550-6) for his postdoctoral assistantship at University of Alberta. The authors also thank Dr. Samira Diar‑Bakirly, a former graduate student at University of Alberta, for providing the gingival fibroblast cells.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2019.104759.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
